author: Augustine of Hippo
book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.

ccel.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

title: Preface to the Anti-Manichæan Writings.

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: No reader of the accompanying volume can be expected to take a very lively interest in its contents, unless he has before his mind some facts regarding the extraordinary genius to whom the heresy of Manichæism owes its origin and its name.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: His history is involved in considerable obscurity, owing to the suspicious nature of the documents from which it is derived, and the difficulty of constructing a consistent and probable account out of the contradictory statements of the Asiatics and the Greeks.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: The ascertained facts, therefore, are few, and may be briefly stated.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: According to the Chronicle of Edessa, Mani was born A.D. 240.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: From his original name, Corbicius or Carcubius, Beausobre conjectures that he was born in Carcub, a town of Chaldæa.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: He belonged to a Magian family, and while still a youth won a distinguished place among the sages of Persia.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: He was master of all the lore peculiar to his class, and was, besides, so proficient a mathematician and geographer, that he was able to construct a globe.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: He was a skilled musician, and had some knowledge of the Greek language,—an accomplishment rare among his countrymen.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: But his fame, and even his ultimate success as a teacher, was due in great measure to his skill in painting, which was so considerable as to earn for him among the Persians the distinctive title, Mani the painter.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: His disposition was ardent and lively but patient and self-restrained.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: His appearance was striking, as he wore the usual dress of a Persian sage: the high-soled shoes, the one red, the other green; the mantle of azure blue, that changed color as he moved; the ebony staff in his right hand, and the Babylonish book under his left arm.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: The meaning of his name, Mani, Manes, or Manichæus, has been the subject of endless conjectures.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: Epiphanius supposes that he was providentially so named, that men might be warned against the mania of his heresy.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: Hyde, whose opinion on any Oriental subject must have weight, tells us that in Persian mani means painter, and that he was so called from his profession.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: Archbishop Usher conjectured that it was a form of Manaem or Menahem, which means Paraclete or Comforter; founding this conjecture on the fact that Sulpicius Severus calls the Israelitish king Menahem, Mane.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: Gataker supplements this idea by the conjecture that Mani took this name at his own instance, and in pursuance of his claim to be the Paraclete.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: It is more probable that, if his name was really given on account of this meaning, he received it from the widow who seems to have adopted him when a boy, and may have called him her Consolation.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: But it is also possible that Mani was not an uncommon Persian name, and that he adopted it for some reason too trifling to discover.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: While still a young man he was ordained as a Christian priest, and distinguished himself in that capacity by his knowledge of Scripture, and the zeal with which he discharged his sacred functions.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: His heretical tendencies, however, were very soon manifested, stimulated, we may suppose, by his anxiety to make the Christian religion more acceptable to those who adhered to the Eastern systems.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: Excommunicated from the Christian Church, Mani found asylum with Sapor, and won his confidence by presenting only the Magian side of his system.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: But no sooner did he permit the Christian element to appear, and call himself the apostle of the Lord, and show a desire to reform Magianism, than his sovereign determined to put him to death as a revolutionist.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: Forced to flee, he took refuge in Turkestan, and gained influence there, partly by decorating the temples with paintings.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: To lend his doctrines the appearance of divine authority, he adopted the same device as Zoroaster and Mohammed.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: Having discovered a cave through which there ran a rill of water, he laid up in it a store of provisions, and retired there for a year, giving out that he was on a visit to heaven.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: In this retirement he produced his Gospel,—a work illustrated with symbolical drawings the ingenuity of which has been greatly praised.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: This book Mani presented to Hormizdas, the son and successor of Sapor, who professed himself favorable to his doctrine, and even built him a castle as a place of shelter and retirement.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: Unfortunately for Mani, Hormizdas died in the second year of his reign; and though his successor, Varanes, was at first willing to shield him from persecution, yet, finding that the Magians were alarmed for their religion, he appointed a disputation to be held between the opposing parties.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: Such trials of dialectic in Eastern courts have not unfrequently resulted in very serious consequences to the parties engaged in them.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: In this instance the result was fatal to Mani.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: Worsted in argument, he was condemned to die, and thus perished in some sense as a martyr.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: The mode of his death is uncertain, but it seems that his skin was stuffed with chaff, and hung up in public in terrorem.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: This occurred in the year 277, and the anniversary was commemorated as the great religious festival of the Manichæans.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: This is not the place to attempt any account or criticism of the strange eclecticism of Mani.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: An adequate idea of the system may be gathered from the accompanying treatises.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: It may, however, be desirable to give some account of the original sources of information regarding it.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: We study the systems of heresiarchs at a disadvantage when our only means of ascertaining their opinions is from the fragmentary quotations and hostile criticism which occur in the writings of their adversaries.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: Such, however, is our only source of information regarding the teaching of Mani.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: Originally, indeed, this heresy was specially active in a literary direction, assailing the Christian Scriptures with an ingenuity of unbelief worthy of a later age, and apparently ambitious of promulgating a rival canon.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: Certainly the writings of its early supporters were numerous; and from the care and elegance with which they were transcribed, the sumptuous character of the manuscripts, and the mysterious emblems with which they were adorned, we should fancy it was intended to inspire the people with respect for an authoritative though as yet undefined code.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: It is, indeed, nowhere said or implied that the sacred books of the Manichæans were reserved for the eye only of the initiated or elect; and their reception of the New Testament Scriptures (subject to their own revision and emendation) would make it difficult for them to establish any secret code apart from these writings.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: They were certainly, however, doctrines of an esoteric kind, which were not divulged to the catechumens or hearers; and many of their books, being written in Persian, Syriac, or Greek, were practically unavailable for the instruction of the Latin speaking population.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: It was not always easy, therefore, to obtain an accurate knowledge of their opinions.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: Commentaries on the whole of the Old and New Testaments were written by Hierax; a Theosophy by Aristocritus; a book of memoirs, or rather Memorabilia, of Mani, and other works, by Heraclides, Aphthonius, Adas, and Agapius.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: Unfortunately all of these books have perished, whether in the flames to which the Christian authorities commanded that all Manichæan books should be consigned, or by the slower if not more critical and impartial processes of time.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: Mani himself was the author of several works: a Gospel, the Treasury of Life (and probably an abridgment of the same), the Mysteries, the Foundation Fpistle, a book of Articles or heads of doctrine, one or two works on astronomy or astrology, and a collection of letters so dangerous, that Manichæans who sought restoration to the Church were required to anathematize them.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: Probably the most important of these writings was the Foundation Epistle, so called because it contained the leading articles of doctrine on which the new system was built.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: This letter was written in Greek or Syriac; but a Latin version of it was current in Africa, and came into the hands of Augustin, who undertook its refutation.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: To accomplish this with the greater precision and effect, he quotes the entire text of each passage of the Epistle before proceeding to criticise it.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: Had Augustin accomplished the whole of his task, we should accordingly have been in possession of the whole of this important document.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: Unfortunately, for reasons unknown, Augustin stops short at an early point in the Epistle; and though he tells us he had notes on the remainder, and would some day expand and publish them, this promise lay unredeemed for thirty years till the day of his death.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: Extracts from the same Epistle and from the Treasury are also given by Augustin in the treatise De Natura Boni.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: Next, we have in the Opus Imperfectum of Augustin some extracts from a letter of Mani to Menoch, which Julian had unearthed and republished to convict Augustin of being still tainted with Manichæan sentiments.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: These extracts give us some insight into the heresiarch’s opinions regarding the corruption of nature and the evils of sexual love.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: Again, we have Mani’s letter to Marcel, preserved by Epiphanius, and given in full by Beausobre; which, however, merely reiterates two of the doctrines most certainly identified with Mani,—the assertion of two principles, and the tenet that the Son of God was man only in appearance.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: Finally, Fabricius has inserted in the fifth volume of his Bibliotheca Græca the fragments, such as they are, collected by Grabe.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: Such is the fragmentary character of the literary remains of Mani: for fuller information regarding his opinions we must depend on Theodoret, Epiphanius, Alexander of Lycopolis, Titus of Bostra, and Augustin.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: Beausobre is of opinion that the Fathers derived all that they knew of Manichæus from the Acts of Archelaus.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: This professes to be a report of a disputation held between Manes and Archelaus, bishop of Caschar in Mesopotamia.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: Grave doubts have been cast on the authenticity of this document, and Burton and Milman seem inclined to consider it an imaginary dialogue, and use it on the understanding that while some of its statements are manifestly untrustworthy, a discriminating reader may gather from it some reliable material.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: In the works of Augustin there are some other pieces which may well be reckoned among the original sources.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: In the reply to Faustus, which is translated in this volume, the book of Faustus is not indeed reproduced; but there is no reason for doubting that his arguments are fairly represented, and we think there is evidence that even the original expression of them is preserved.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: Augustin had been acquainted with Faustus for many years.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: He first met him at Carthage in 383, and found him nothing more than a clever and agreeable talker, making no pretension to science or philosophy, and with only slender reading.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: His cleverness is sufficiently apparent in his debate with Augustin; the objections he leads are plausible, and put with acuteness, but at the same time with a flippancy which betrays a want of earnestness and real interest in the questions.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: In his reply to Faustus, Augustin is very much on the defensive, and his statements are apologetic rather than systematic.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: But in an age when the ability to read was by no means commensurate with the interest taken in theological questions, written discussions were necessarily supplemented by public disputations.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: These theological contests seem to have been a popular entertainment in North Africa; the people attending in immense crowds, while reporters took down what was said on either side for the sake of appeal as well as for the information of the absent.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: In two such disputations Augustin engaged in connection with Manichæism.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: The first was held on the 28th and 29th of August, 392, with a Manichæan priest, Fortunatus.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: To this encounter Augustin was invited by a deputation of Donatists and Catholics, who were alike alarmed at the progress which this heresy was making in the district of Hippo.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: Fortunatus at first showed some reluctance to meet so formidable an antagonist, but was prevailed upon by his own sectaries, and shows no nervousness during the debate.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: His incompetence, however, was manifest to the Manichæans themselves; and so hopeless was it to think of any further proselytizing in Hippo, that he left that city, and was too much ashamed of himself ever to return.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: The character of his reasoning is shifty; he evades Augustin’s questions and starts fresh ones.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: Augustin pushes his usual and fundamental objection to the Manichæan system.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: If God is impassable and incorruptible, how could He be injured by the assaults of the kingdom of darkness?

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: In opposition to the statement of Fortunatus, that the Almighty produces no evil, he explains that God made no nature evil, but made man free, and that voluntary sin is the grand original evil.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: The most remarkable circumstance in the discussion is the desire of Fortunatus to direct the conversation to the conduct of the Manichæans, and the refusal of Augustin to make good the charges which had been made against them, or to discuss anything but the doctrine.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: Twelve years after this, a similar disputation was held between Augustin and one of the elect among the Manichæans, who had come to Hippo to propagate his religion.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: This man, Felix, is described by Augustin as being ill-educated, but more adroit and subtle than Fortunatus.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: After a keen discussion, which occupied two days, the proceedings terminated by Felix signing a recantation of his errors in the form of an anathema on Mani, his doctrines, and the seducing spirit that possessed him.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: These two disputations are valuable, as exhibiting the points of the Manichæan system to which its own adherents were accustomed to direct attention, and the arguments on which they specially relied for their support.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: The works given in the accompanying volume comprehend by no means the whole of Augustin’s writings against this heresy.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: Before his ordination he wrote five anti-Manichæan books, entitled, De Libero Arbitrio, De Genesi contra Manichæos, De Moribus Ecclesiæ Catholicæ, De Moribus Manichæorum, and De Vera Religione.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: These Paulinus called his anti-Manichæan Pentateuch.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: After his ordination he was equally diligent, publishing a little treatise in the year 391, under the title De Utilitate Credendi, which was immediately followed by a small work, De Duabus Animabus.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: In the following year the report of the Disputatio contra Fortunatum was published; and after this, at short intervals, there appeared the books Contra Adimantum, Contra Epistolam Manichæi quam vocant Fundamenti, Contra Faustum, Disputatio contra Felicem, De Naturo Boni, and Contra Secundinum.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: Besides these writings, which are exclusively occupied with Manichæism, there are others in which the Manichæan doctrines are handled with more or less directness.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: These are the Confessions, the 79th and 236th Letters, the Lecture on Psalm 140, Sermons 1, 2, 12, 50, 153, 182, 237, the Liber de Agone Christiano, and the De Continentia.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: Of these writings, Augustin himself professed a preference for the reply to the letter of Secundinus.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: It is a pleasing feature of the times, that a heretic whom he did not know even by sight should write to Augustin entreating him to abstain from writing against the Manichæans, and reconsider his position, and ally himself with those whom he had till now fancied to be in error.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: His language is respectful, and illustrates the esteem in which Augustin was held by his contemporaries; though he does not scruple to insinuate that his conversion from Manichæism was due to motives not of the highest kind.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: We have not given this letter and its reply, because the preference of Augustin has not been ratified by the judgment of his readers.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: The present volume gives a fair sample of Augustin’s controversial powers.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: His nine years’ personal experience of the vanity of Manichæism made him thoroughly earnest and sympathetic in his efforts to disentangle other men from its snares, and also equipped him with the knowledge requisite for this task.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: No doubt the Pelagian controversy was more congenial to his mind.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: His logical acuteness and knowledge of Scripture availed him more in combating men who fought with the same weapons, than in dealing with a system which threw around its positions the mist of Gnostic speculation, or veiled its doctrine under a grotesque mythology, or based itself on a cosmogony too fantastic for a Western mind to tolerate.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: But however Augustin may have misconceived the strange forms in which this system was presented, there is no doubt that he comprehended and demolished its fundamental principles; that he did so as a necessary part of his own personal search for the truth; and that in doing so he gained possession, vitally and permanently of ideas and principles which subsequently entered into all he thought and wrote.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: In finding his way through the mazes of the obscure region into which Mani had led him, he once for all ascertained the true relation subsisting between God and His creatures, formed his opinion regarding the respective provinces of reason and faith, and the connection of the Old and New Testaments, and found the root of all evil in the created will.

verses.book: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
verses.text: Some knowledge of the Magianism of the time of Mani may be obtained from the sacred books of the Parsis, especially from the Vendidad Sade, an account of which is given by Dr. Wilson, of Bombay, in his book on the Parsi Religion.—Tr.
